summary: This guest post (by George Chamales, introduced by Charity Majors) provides a practical framework for engineers trying to champion third-party observability and SaaS tools within organizations that have security and compliance concerns. It acknowledges that both the champion's optimism and the security team's caution are valid, and offers a structured worksheet for evaluating risks vs. rewards when proposing a new external service.
key_points: Both engineering and security perspectives on third-party services are valid — the rewards are real, but so are the risks
Security certifications and polished security pages are not reliable indicators of actual security posture
The risk of a third-party service is highly dependent on how you use it and what data you send
Operational metrics are categorically less sensitive than PII/PHI, which changes the risk calculus
A structured worksheet approach helps put risks and rewards in context for management and security teams
money_quotes: To you, it's an opportunity to deploy new features in a flash, juice your team's productivity, and save boatloads of money. To your security and compliance teams, it's a chance to lose your customers' data, cause your applications to fall over, and do inordinate damage to your company's reputation and bottom line.
The good news is, you're absolutely right. The bad news is, so are they.
History is full of compromised companies who had very pretty security pages and certifications.

There's risk no matter what you do – bringing in the service is risky, doing nothing is risky. You can only mitigate risks up to a point. Beyond that point, it's the rewards that make risks worthwhile.
Outsourcing your observability to a vendor for whom it's their One Job is almost always the right call, in terms of money and time and focus — and yes, even security.
key_insight: Successfully championing a third-party service requires acknowledging that security teams' concerns are legitimate while building a structured case that the rewards outweigh the risks — and that doing nothing carries its own risks.
